Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Class AvaloniaRefreshTicker

Namespace
Pandowdy.UI
Assembly
Pandowdy.UI.dll

Avalonia-based implementation of IRefreshTicker providing 60 Hz UI refresh timing.

public sealed class AvaloniaRefreshTicker : IRefreshTicker
Inheritance
object
AvaloniaRefreshTicker
Implements
IRefreshTicker
Inherited Members
object.Equals(object)
object.Equals(object, object)
object.GetHashCode()
object.GetType()
object.ReferenceEquals(object, object)
object.ToString()

Remarks

Purpose: Provides a 60 Hz timing signal for driving UI-based frame rendering and updates. Uses Avalonia's DispatcherTimer to ensure all timing events occur on the UI thread.

Implementation: Uses Rx.NET's Subject pattern to create an observable stream of timestamps. The DispatcherTimer publishes to this stream at approximately 60 Hz (every ~16.67ms).

Thread Safety: All timer callbacks execute on the Avalonia UI thread via DispatcherTimer, ensuring safe interaction with UI elements without explicit marshaling.

Lifecycle: The timer starts when Start() is called and runs continuously until the application closes. The Stop() method is currently a no-op as Avalonia's DispatcherTimer automatically stops when the application shuts down.

Usage Pattern: 

var ticker = new AvaloniaRefreshTicker();
ticker.Stream.Subscribe(timestamp => RefreshDisplay());
ticker.Start(); // Begin emitting 60 Hz updates

Constructors

AvaloniaRefreshTicker()

Initializes a new instance of the AvaloniaRefreshTicker class.

public AvaloniaRefreshTicker()

Remarks

Rx.NET Pattern: Creates a Subject that acts as both an observer (for publishing events) and an observable (for subscribers to receive events).

Stream Setup: The Subject is wrapped in AsObservable() to prevent external code from directly calling OnNext/OnError/OnCompleted on the Subject. This encapsulation ensures only this class can publish events.

No Timer Start: The constructor does not start the timer automatically. Call Start() to begin emitting timing events.

Properties

Stream

Gets the observable stream that emits timestamps at approximately 60 Hz.

public IObservable<DateTime> Stream { get; }

Property Value

IObservable<DateTime>

An IObservable<T> that emits UTC timestamps each time the ticker fires.

Remarks

Frequency: Emits approximately 60 times per second (every ~16.67 milliseconds) when the ticker is running.

Thread Context: All emissions occur on the Avalonia UI thread, making it safe to update UI elements directly from subscribers without additional marshaling.

Timestamp Format: Provides DateTime.UtcNow at each emission. Subscribers can use these timestamps for time-based calculations or simply as periodic triggers.

Subscription Example: 

ticker.Stream
    .Subscribe(timestamp => 
    {
        // This executes on UI thread at ~60 Hz
        UpdateDisplay();
    });

Methods

Start()

Starts the refresh ticker, causing it to begin emitting periodic timing signals at 60 Hz.

public void Start()

Remarks

Timer Setup: Uses Avalonia's DispatcherTimer.Run() with a 16.67ms interval (1/60 second) to achieve approximately 60 Hz refresh rate.

Callback Behavior: The timer callback returns true to indicate it should continue running. Each callback publishes the current UTC time to the stream via _subject.OnNext(DateTime.UtcNow).

Idempotency: Calling Start() multiple times will create multiple timer instances. This is generally not recommended but won't cause crashes. Consider adding a guard flag if multiple Start() calls are a concern.

Thread Affinity: All timer callbacks execute on the Avalonia UI dispatcher thread, ensuring safe UI interaction without explicit Invoke() calls.

Timing Accuracy: The actual interval may vary slightly based on UI thread load and system scheduling, but should maintain an average close to 60 Hz for smooth animation.

Stop()

Stops the refresh ticker. Currently a no-op implementation.

public void Stop()

Remarks

Current Implementation: This method is currently a no-op (does nothing). The DispatcherTimer will automatically stop when the Avalonia application closes.

Future Enhancement: A proper implementation would:

  • Store the timer handle returned by DispatcherTimer.Run()
  • Call timer.Stop() or dispose the handle to halt timing events
  • Potentially call _observer.OnCompleted() to signal stream completion

Current Behavior: Timers continue running until application shutdown. For most use cases (single ticker for application lifetime), this is acceptable.

Memory Implications: If creating/disposing multiple tickers during application lifetime, the lack of Stop() implementation could result in multiple timer instances running simultaneously.
